title: Five key takeaways from the Clintons' Epstein testimonies
author: Lucia Stein
fullText: The recorded depositions of former US president Bill Clinton and former secretary of state Hillary Clinton feature questions about convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, his victims and the people he once associated with.The US House Oversight Committee released videos of the testimonies, which are both more than four hours long, as part of its investigation into the disgraced financier and his ties to prominent public figures.Mr Clinton was subpoenaed to appear after featuring prominently in the first release of select "Epstein files" documents last year, including in undated photographs with Epstein and his accomplice, Ghislaine Maxwell.His deposition took place a day after his wife, Hillary Clinton, appeared before the committee. The two Clintons were first questioned by the Republicans, because they have the majority in the committee, and then the Democrats, alternating until the sessions wrapped up.These were the key takeaways from their testimony.Bill Clinton asked about Epstein friendshipEarly in his testimony to Congress, Bill Clinton was asked to describe the nature of his relationship with Mr Epstein.Clinton testified that he took four or five trips to Asia and Africa and one to Northern Europe on Epstein's plane, as well as a flight from Florida to New York. He said he was unaware of Epstein's crimes at the time.Mr Clinton also said that his former economic adviser Larry Summers spoke to him about Epstein some time after he left the White House.He said Summers described Epstein in a late 2001 or 2002 call as an "information hungry person" who had made a commitment of "several million dollars" to brain research.Summers said Epstein wanted to discuss economics and politics with Mr Clinton."He said 'he's got this massive aeroplane' and he would take my staff, secret service detail, and anybody else you wanted to bring on the trips that he knew I was planning to set up a global network to provide lifesaving AIDS medicine to as many people as possible, as quickly as possible," he said."…That's why when Larry Summers said this, I thought, 'boy, this would be great'. And so we followed up and took the trips."After emails detailing Summers' relationship with Epstein were released in November, the former economic adviser resigned from teaching at Harvard.Clinton was also asked during his testimony when his relationship with Epstein ended and replied that he believed it ended in 2003.The former president said it ended after he met donors that were more suited to support his charity work for the Clinton Global Initiative."I didn't think he was really interested in what I was doing, that is he asked all these questions. He did ask about economics and politics as he said he would and he never asked me anything untoward," he said."It's just that … I thought I had done what I promised to and he had done what he promised to do."And I'd always, over the years with these donors, tried not to overstay my welcome."I thought that we'd done enough and it was time to start working with other people."Bill Clinton discusses Trump conversationMr Clinton was asked at one point in his testimony if US President Donald Trump should be compelled to testify, to which he says it is not for him to decide.Mr Clinton spoke of a conversation he once had with Mr Trump. (Reuters: Jonathan Ernst)He added that Mr Trump "did know him well" and started recounting a conversation he had with Mr Trump about Epstein around 2002 or 2003.It occurred at one of the president's golf courses during a fundraising event."[Trump] never said anything to me to make me think he was involved in anything improper with regard to Epstein either. He just didn't," he testified."He just said: 'We were friends and then we had a falling out over a land deal, property deal.' That's all."Mr Trump was a friend of Epstein's in the 1990s and early 2000s, but the president has said they fell out some time before Epstein was first arrested.Trump has consistently denied any wrongdoing in relation to Epstein.Mr Clinton added later in the deposition that Mr Trump never put any "sexual spin" on conversations that involved Epstein.Bill Clinton asked about Virginia Roberts GiuffreAt one point Democrat Melanie Stansbury, a representative of New Mexico's first congressional district, discussed testimony provided by Epstein's victims.Former president Bill Clinton testified behind closed doors to a congressional panel. (Reuters: GOP Oversight/Handout)Among them was Maria Farmer, who was employed by the financier and abused by him for many years, and who stated that Mr Clinton had visited Epstein's house three to four times while he was a sitting president between 1995-1996.When asked if he had visited Epstein's house while he was president, Mr Clinton replied: "I do not remember doing that. I do not believe I did."Ms Stansbury also asked Mr Clinton if he was familiar with Virginia Roberts Giuffre, a prominent Epstein accuser who wrote a memoir last year about his and Maxwell's abuse of her.She died by suicide in Western Australia in April.Clinton nodded yes.Ms Stansbury then asked him about a diary entry from Roberts Giuffre that was shared with the panel by the victim's family."In it is a quote wherein she writes 'Bill Clinton met two times island with two girls CTGM and JE'. What is your reaction to that?"It's not true," Mr Clinton said before his lawyer interjected to ask if there were any dates associated with the entry."Did she say that in her book?" Mr Clinton asked.He was told this passage was from her personal diary that was shared with the panel by her family."But the book came out more recently. Did she say that in her book?" Mr Clinton asked.He was told by Ms Stansbury that she was just reading an entry from Roberts Giuffre's diary that her family shared."I think she ultimately reached a different conclusion although I had no contact with her," Mr Clinton said.Hillary Clinton asked about a long list of peopleIn her deposition, Ms Clinton denied ever meeting Epstein."I do not recall ever encountering Mr Epstein. I never flew on his plane, or visited his island, homes or offices," Ms Clinton said."Like every decent person, I've been horrified by what we have learned about their crimes."  She was also asked if she had ever communicated about Epstein or Maxwell with a long list of names, including Noam Chomsky, Harvey Weinstein, Bill Gates, Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor and Peter Mandelson.She replied that she had not to most of them.When asked if she had ever spoken to former White House strategist Steve Bannon about the pair, she replied: "I don't know if I've ever met him, you'll have to introduce me.""To be absolutely clear, you're asking me if I've ever communicated about Jeffrey Epstein — a man I never met — with any of these people, most of whom I've never heard of and certainly not met?" she then asked.A member of the committee replied they were asking if she'd met "Mr Epstein or Mrs Maxwell"."You did not say that," she replied."That's OK. I didn't talk about her with any of these people either, I barely knew her."Hillary Clinton storms out, fends off intrusive questionsAbout 60 minutes into her deposition, Ms Clinton stormed out of the room after she was told by one of her lawyers that a photo from inside the room has been leaked on social media.The deposition was a closed-door hearing, so her lawyer asked how this photo was allowed and who shared it.One of the politicians in the room said they had shared a photo on their social media, saying it was taken before the hearing began."I am done with this," Ms Clinton interrupted."If you guys are doing that, I am done. You can hold me in contempt from now until the cows come home. This is just typical behaviour."I am done," she added before standing up from her chair.The video then goes black with the words "off the record" on the screen before the session resumes.At other times during her appearance, Ms Clinton was subjected to sensational questions about her opinion and feelings upon seeing images of her husband in the Epstein files.She fended them off saying she was not going to speculate or offer her opinions.Republican Lauren Boebert also asked Ms Clinton about "Pizzagate", a debunked conspiracy theory that a paedophilia ring linked to the former presidential candidate's inner circle was operating out of a Washington DC pizzeria."Pizzagate was totally made up. It was an outrageous allegation that ended up hurting a number of people," Ms Clinton said."I can't believe you're even referencing it," she added.After Boebert asked a few more questions about Pizzagate, Ms Clinton responded: "I mean, I expected a lot of interesting questions today, but Pizzagate was not on my list."After her deposition, Ms Clinton criticised the committee's "repetitive" and "unproductive" questioning, its refusal to open the hearing to media, and its failure to force US President Donald Trump to answer questions.Democrats on the committee have said they would keep pushing to depose Mr Trump as well.
summary: More than nine hours of footage of Bill and Hillary Clinton's depositions on their alleged ties to Jeffrey Epstein have been released. Here are the key takeaways.
